---
title: "HW 6 example"
output: html_document
date: "2026-05-14"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

```{r}
install.packages(c("sf", "ggplot2", "ggspatial", "tidycensus", "tmap"))
```
```{r}
library(tidycensus)
```

```{r}
library(sf)
```

## Overview

In this assignment you will:

1. Install and load packages for spatial data analysis
2. Download real U.S. Census data directly into R using `tidycensus`
3. Create choropleth maps of two demographic variables
4. Compare the two maps and interpret what you see

---

## Part 1: Install & Load Packages

Run this once to install the packages (you only need to do this one time):

```{r install, eval=FALSE}
install.packages(c("tidycensus", "sf", "ggplot2", "tmap", "patchwork", "dplyr"))
```

Now load them:

```{r load-packages}
library(tidycensus)   # Download Census data + shapefiles
library(sf)           # Work with spatial (map) data
library(ggplot2)      # General plotting
library(tmap)         # Map-specific plotting
library(patchwork)    # Arrange multiple plots side by side
library(dplyr)        # Data wrangling
```

---

## Part 2: Get a Census API Key

`tidycensus` pulls data directly from the U.S. Census Bureau. To use it,
you need a **free API key**:

1. Go to <https://api.census.gov/data/key_signup.html>
2. Enter your name and email — your key will be emailed to you
3. Paste it below (replace `"YOUR_KEY_HERE"` with your actual key):

```{r api-key, eval=FALSE}
census_api_key("YOUR_KEY_HERE", install = TRUE)
# After running this once, restart R and you won't need to run it again
```

---

## Part 3: Explore Available Variables

The Census tracks hundreds of variables. Use `load_variables()` to browse them.
The American Community Survey 5-year estimates (`acs5`) are the most reliable
for county-level data.

```{r browse-variables, eval=FALSE}
# Load the variable list for the 2022 5-year ACS
acs_vars <- load_variables(2022, "acs5", cache = TRUE)

# Open an interactive search window
View(acs_vars)

# Or search by keyword
acs_vars |>
  filter(grepl("median household income", label, ignore.case = TRUE)) |>
  select(name, label, concept)
```

**Commonly used variable codes:**

| Variable Code | What it measures |
|---------------|-----------------|
| `B19013_001`  | Median household income |
| `B15003_022`  | Bachelor's degree holders (count) |
| `B01003_001`  | Total population |
| `B17001_002`  | Population below poverty level |
| `B08303_001`  | Travel time to work |

---

## Part 4: Download Census Data with Shapefiles

`get_acs()` downloads both the data **and** the county boundary shapes in one step.
The key arguments are:

- `geography`: level of geography (`"county"`, `"state"`, `"tract"`, etc.)
- `variables`: the variable code(s) you want
- `state`: filter to one state (use 2-letter abbreviation)
- `year`: which ACS year (2022 is the most recent stable release)
- `geometry = TRUE`: this is what adds the map shapes!

```{r download-data}
# Download median household income by county for Washington State
income_data <- get_acs(
  geography = "county",
  variables = "B19013_001",   # Median household income
  state     = "WA",           # Change this to your state!
  year      = 2022,
  geometry  = TRUE            # Includes county boundary shapefiles
)

# Preview the data
head(income_data)
```

What do the columns mean?

- `GEOID`: unique identifier for each county
- `NAME`: county name
- `variable`: the variable code you requested
- `estimate`: the actual value (e.g., median income in dollars)
- `moe`: margin of error
- `geometry`: the map shape for each county

```{r download-data-2}
# Download a second variable: % of adults with a bachelor's degree
# We need two variables: bachelor's degree holders AND total adults 25+
education_data <- get_acs(
  geography = "county",
  variables = c(
    bachelors = "B15003_022",   # Count: bachelor's degree
    total_pop = "B15003_001"    # Count: total adults 25+
  ),
  state    = "WA",
  year     = 2022,
  geometry = TRUE
)

# The data comes in "long" format (one row per variable per county).
# We need to reshape it to "wide" format and calculate a percentage.
education_wide <- education_data |>
  select(GEOID, NAME, variable, estimate, geometry) |>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from  = variable,
    values_from = estimate
  ) |>
  mutate(pct_bachelors = (bachelors / total_pop) * 100)  # Calculate percentage

head(education_wide)
```

---

## Part 5: Make Your Maps

### Map 1 — Median Household Income

```{r map-income}
map_income <- ggplot(data = income_data) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = estimate), color = "white", size = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option = "plasma",
    labels = scales::dollar,     # Format numbers as $XX,XXX
    name   = "Median Income"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Median Household Income by County",
    subtitle = "Washington State, 2022 ACS 5-Year Estimates",
    caption  = "Source: U.S. Census Bureau via tidycensus"
  ) +
  theme_void() +                 # Clean look — removes axes/grid
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

map_income
```

### Map 2 — Educational Attainment

```{r map-education}
map_education <- ggplot(data = education_wide) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = pct_bachelors), color = "white", size = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option = "mako",
    labels = function(x) paste0(round(x, 1), "%"),
    name   = "% Bachelor's+"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Adults with Bachelor's Degree or Higher",
    subtitle = "Washington State, 2022 ACS 5-Year Estimates",
    caption  = "Source: U.S. Census Bureau via tidycensus"
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

map_education
```

### Side-by-Side Comparison

```{r side-by-side, fig.width=12, fig.height=5}
map_income + map_education
```

---

## Part 6: Bonus — Interactive Maps with tmap

`tmap` can make interactive maps your readers can zoom and hover on:

```{r interactive-maps, eval=FALSE}
tmap_mode("view")   # Switch to interactive mode

tm_shape(income_data) +
  tm_polygons(
    col         = "estimate",
    palette     = "plasma",
    title       = "Median Household Income",
    popup.vars  = c("County" = "NAME", "Income" = "estimate")
  )
```


```{r}
library(tmap)

tmap_mode("plot")

map1 <- tm_shape
```
